headline: Gap: most guides ignore how to tie schema to the same source data as page content
name: Gap: most guides ignore how to tie schema to the same source data as page content
description: Many businesses generate schema from page templates, but few explain how to keep that schema synchronized with the same data used to render the visible page. Th
articleBody: Many businesses generate schema from page templates, but few explain how to keep that schema synchronized with the same data used to render the visible page. The best practice is to bind JSON-LD generation to the CMS or application data model, not to copied text in the template. That means the page title, service name, author, price, rating, date, address, and FAQ content should all come from the same source fields that populate the page body. If the content changes, the schema should change automatically at build time or render time. This avoids one of the most common failures in per-page schema generation: stale markup that says one thing while the page says another. For larger sites, use typed objects or schema definitions in code so required properties cannot be omitted silently. Add validation in CI so broken or incomplete JSON-LD is caught before deployment. If you already run a headless CMS, the schema layer should be a thin transformation from content fields to Schema.org fields, not a manual editing step. The more the schema is hand-maintained, the more drift you will get after content updates, seasonal changes, or editorial rewrites.
keywords: Per-page JSON-LD website schema generation, procedural
